focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 2

Nate was standing in the doorway of the guest room in his boxers and a podcast-branded T-shirt, and Paul knew before Nate said anything because Nate wasn't performing. Nate was always performing, at the draft table, at the grill, on the phone to his eleven listeners, in his sleep for all Paul knew. But the man in the doorway was small and still and his mouth was working without sound, and that was how Paul Decker learned that his best friend was dead.

"Something's wrong with Martin." Nate's voice came out at a register Paul had never heard from him — flat, airless, like the words had been squeezed through a slit. "I went up to — I was gonna ask him about the side bet, and he's — Paul, I think he's —"

Paul was already moving. Bare feet on the cold hallway floor, the bathroom nightlight throwing that sick yellow glow Martin always left on for guests who drank too much and needed a beacon to piss by. Martin's bedroom door was open at the end of the hall. Nate must have left it open. The light from the nightstand lamp spilled into the hallway in a thin stripe.

Martin was in bed, turned onto his left side, facing the window. The sheet had twisted down around his hips. His left arm was trapped under him. His right hand hung off the mattress, fingers open toward the floor. His mouth was open. His eyes were closed. Paul would be grateful for that later, grateful he didn't have to see whatever the eyes looked like, but right now he wasn't thinking about later. The color of Martin's face was wrong. Martin was always ruddy. Flushed from lake sun, from booze, from yelling about someone's draft pick, from the blood pressure he joked about and never treated. Now his skin was a grayish yellow that Paul had never seen on a living person, and the seeing of it was what to...
open_hooks_to_carry: Establish all seven men and their group dynamics through the draft
Martin's controlling generosity and quiet authority over the group
Tommy gives Martin the Viagra as a running joke
Joel's visible anger that no one can identify the source of
Nate records portions of the evening for his podcast
Cliff makes pointed comments about money Martin owes him
Doug's quiet self-deprecation and tendency to disappear into the background
Nate discovers Martin dead at chapter's end
secondary_character_beats: Doug should have a moment of genuine warmth that establishes him as someone who came to this weekend because he needed people
Cliff's deal-making language in the draft should feel like second nature, not a villainous tell

setups_to_plant.description: Tommy gives Martin a Viagra pill as a joke about Martin's age — played for laughs, with no hint it matters medically
Joel and Martin argue heatedly late at night; Joel refuses to explain to anyone what it was about
Nate records portions of draft night on his phone for podcast content — ambient audio that will later prove evidentially relevant
Subtle moments of Martin being unusually protective or deferential to Tommy — making sure Tommy's glass is full, including Tommy in conversations Tommy tries to leave, a private word on the deck

state_deltas_required: twenty-five years of accumulated grudges compressed into one evening
Martin is found dead — the ritual weekend becomes a crime scene
